headline: Palo Alto Networks Raises Profit Outlook, Citing AI Demand
article_title: Palo Alto Networks Raises Profit Outlook, Citing AI Demand

article_items.title: Palo Alto Networks Raises Profit Outlook, Citing AI Demand
article_items.description: Palo Alto Networks Inc. released a forecast for adjusted earnings that was stronger than anticipated, signaling ongoing demand for security services as artificial intelligence-related threats continue to raise concerns for companies and governments.
article_items.excerpt: Palo Alto Networks Inc. released a forecast for adjusted earnings that was stronger than anticipated, signaling ongoing demand for security services as artificial intelligence-related threats continue to raise concerns for companies and governments.
article_items.summary: released a forecast for adjusted earnings that was stronger than anticipated, signaling ongoing demand for security services as artificial intelligence-related threats continue to raise concerns for companies and governments. It also raised its full-year adjusted profit outlook to $3.77 to $3.79 a share, up from its prior forecast of $3.65 to $3.70. The cybersecurity company sees adjusted earnings of 96 cents to 98 cents a share for the next quarter, above the average analyst estimate of 94 cents. article_items.title: Eight PC brands commit to Nvidia-MediaTek RTX Spark as AI agent laptops take shape for fall
article_items.description: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang unveiled RTX Spark at Computex Taipei this morning, announcing a new superchip designed to power a generation of Windows laptops and compact desktops built around on-device AI agents. The announcement, made at GTC Taipei on May 31, comes as Computex 2026 opens in Taipei with AI hardware dominating the show floor. Devices from major PC manufacturers are expected to ship this fall.

article_items.title: Three Steps to Start Integrating AI and AI Agents Into Your Marketing Workflows
article_items.description: Own the playbook, keep an expert in the loop, don't give sacrifice good for perfection. These are three key steps to operationalizing AI for B2B Marketers.
article_items.excerpt: Own the playbook, keep an expert in the loop, don't give sacrifice good for perfection. These are three key steps to operationalizing AI for B2B Marketers.
article_items.summary: The top two answers weren't about tools or models but about execution: 58% want training on integrating AI into their existing workflows. Three Steps to Start Integrating AI and AI Agents Into Your Marketing Workflows Our 2026 State of AI for Business Report surveyed more than 2,100 business professionals - 86% of whom are B2B marketers - and asked what AI training they want most. The tools, with human oversight, will help you get there. She’ll share more about how you and your team can build agent-powered workflows that you can trust and scale. The approach, then, is not to get caught up in the tools or expect them to do all the heavy lifting. article_items.title: Trump signs scaled-back AI cybersecurity order - Engadget
article_items.description: AI models can voluntarily submitted to the government for review 30 days before release under Trump's new executive order.
article_items.excerpt: AI models can voluntarily submitted to the government for review 30 days before release under Trump's new executive order.
article_items.summary: AI models can voluntarily submitted to the government for review 30 days before release under Trump's new executive order. "We don't want a situation in which any administration can exercise arbitrary power over whether, when and how models are released, particularly when they could use security as a pretense to block or handicap a model for political or ideological reasons that aren't related," he said. The order tasks the Office of the National Cyber Director , which is responsible for advising the president on cybersecurity matters, with developing a process that would allow the US to share information about software vulnerabilities identified by AI systems like Claude Mythos with operators of critical infrastructure, including banks, local utilities and hospitals, before those models are made publicly available. "We don't want a situation in which any administration can exercise arbitrary power over whether, when and how models are released, particularly when they could use security as a pretense to block or handicap a model.
Trump signs scaled-back AI cybersecurity order The federal government will only have 30 days at best to review new models.
The new directive, signed in a private ceremony, asks some AI companies to share their most powerful models for voluntary government review 30 days before making them available to the public.

An earlier draft had called for giving the government as much as 90 days to review a model, with some industry officials reportedly pushing for that period be shortened to as little as 14 days before today's.
On Tuesday, President Trump signed an executive order calling for the creation of a framework designed to give the federal government the capability to evaluate AI models.
Trump was originally expected to announce the order on May 21, but according to Axios the White House postponed the signing ceremony following pressure from tech industry insiders.

The order tasks the Office of the National Cyber Director , which is responsible for advising the president on cybersecurity matters, with developing a process that would allow the US to share information about software.
"An opaque procedure allows for that possibility." That Trump has decided, after his earlier to misgivings, to regulate the AI industry in some form is a departure.
Although he had not seen the final executive order, Jain called the order "opaque" at the time, noting it doesn't give the public much visibility into the benchmarking process.
Prior to the announcement, Engadget spoke to the Center for Democracy and Technology .
The president later told reporters he "didn't like certain aspects" of the original order.

article_items.title: Trump signs executive order seeking early access to new AI releases
article_items.description: Under new rules, tech companies will be asked to share AI models with government for review before public release
article_items.excerpt: Under new rules, tech companies will be asked to share AI models with government for review before public release
article_items.summary: If officials deem the model would, tech companies would provide the federal government with access to these models for a period of up to 30 days before public release. An earlier version of the order reportedly requested the models 90 days before their release. The National Security Agency and the Department of Defense will help determine which AI models need government scrutiny, and the treasury department will play a key role in finding vulnerabilities in AI models. Under the new guidelines, developers would be able to engage the federal government to determine how risky their model may be, and whether it would benefit from the framework. The National Security Agency and the Department of Defense will help determine which AI models need government scrutiny, and the treasury department will play a key role in finding vulnerabilities in AI models.

article_items.title: Microsoft's Surface RTX Spark Dev Box will handle tougher AI workloads - Engadget
article_items.description: Microsoft is making a Surface AI dev desktop for people who don't want a laptop.
article_items.excerpt: Microsoft is making a Surface AI dev desktop for people who don't want a laptop.
article_items.summary: Announced at Build today, it's basically a desktop version of the Laptop Ultimate built for sustained AI work like "long-running training jobs, agentic Al pipelines and local model fine-tuning," according to Microsoft. It'll also have NVIDIA's RTX Blackwell GPU embedded, giving it gaming performance similar to the RTX 5070's laptop version. In addition to the Surface Laptop Ultra , Microsoft has another device coming that's powered by NVIDIA's RTX Spark chip : the creatively named Surface RTX Spark Dev Box. Microsoft's Surface RTX Spark Dev Box will handle tougher AI workloads It's an AI Surface dev box for people who don't want a laptop. Microsoft hasn't listed any pricing for the Surface RTX Spark Dev Box yet, but it says it'll arrive later this year through Microsoft.com. In addition to the Surface Laptop Ultra , Microsoft has another device coming that's powered by NVIDIA's RTX Spark chip : the creatively named Surface RTX Spark Dev Box.

Announced at Build today, it's basically a desktop version of the Laptop Ultimate built for sustained AI work like "long-running training jobs, agentic Al pipelines and local model fine-tuning," according to Microsoft.
Microsoft's Surface RTX Spark Dev Box will handle tougher AI workloads It's an AI Surface dev box for people who don't want a laptop.
You can think of the RTX Spark Dev Box as Microsoft's answer to AMD's Ryzen AI Halo PC and NVIDIA's DGX Spark mini PC , both of which cost a whopping $3,999.
Again, much like the Surface Laptop Ultra, there aren't too many surprises with the Dev Box.
Microsoft is making a Surface AI dev desktop for people who don't want a laptop.
Thanks to the RTX Spark chip, it can have up to a 128GB of unified memory and offers a petaflop worth of AI computing power.
Microsoft hasn't listed any pricing for the Surface RTX Spark Dev Box yet, but it says it'll arrive later this year through Microsoft.com.
Naturally, the Dev Box can also handle more heat, thanks to its 100W thermal envelope (and low power Arm CPUs).
These are systems for developers, after all, despite their gaming abilities.
Don't expect to walk into Best Buy to grab this thing.

article_items.title: Vendor Training Robots With Human Data Raises $60 Million
article_items.description: Vendor Training Robots With Human Data Raises $60 Million
article_items.excerpt: Vendor Training Robots With Human Data Raises $60 Million
article_items.summary: Vendor Training Robots With Human Data Raises $60 Million Vendor Training Robots With Human Data Raises $60 Million The vendor is among a group of companies targeting the fast-growing physical AI market. Startup Mecka AI has raised $60 million to train robots on direct human data. Mecka plans to use the investment to expand its team of data engineers and researchers, bolstering the current staff number of 40. Explaining the company’s vision, Gao said its aim is to become the “data and deployment layer for physical AI .” Mecka plans to take robotics training beyond teleoperation and simulation by using actual human data, sourced using body sensors iPhones and other devices, from a variety of different. The data is then run through the vendor’s internal labs, which build and train computer vision models for human motion tracking and 3D construction.

Startup Mecka AI has raised $60 million to train robots on direct human data.
Mecka plans to use the investment to expand its team of data engineers and researchers, bolstering the current staff number of 40.
The data is then run through the vendor’s internal labs, which build and train computer vision models for human motion tracking and 3D construction.

Explaining the company’s vision, Gao said its aim is to become the “data and deployment layer for physical AI .” Mecka plans to take robotics training beyond teleoperation and simulation by using actual human data.
The vendor, founded in 2025, said it completed the funding round in two parts: a $25 million Series A that closed in November, and then $35 million in follow-on investments.

According to Gao, the concept is gathering momentum, and the vendor is claiming an annual run rate of $100 million based on contracts it has already signed, although it has not yet named any customers.
It’s an unusual path the vendor chose after months of research, visits to labs and canvassing industry opinion.
for 26 years, including spells as editor of leading consumer news website and weekly Auto Express and respected buying guide CarBuyer.
The New York City-based vendor’s CEO, Josh Gao, revealed the investment in an interview with Fortune and a post on X .
The company's valuation has not yet been disclosed.
Graham Hope has worked in automotive journalism in the U.K.
